tenet: Use state machines / typestate — encode a multi-state protocol so illegal operations don't compile
why_discriminating: A three-state protocol (Closed -> Listening -> Established) is harder to fully type-encode than a two-state builder. The naive solution is a runtime state enum where every operation is always callable; the skillful solution is typestate where `send` exists only on an established connection. This is the 'hard' task expected to leave headroom: even with the skill Claude may only partially typestate it (e.g. protect send but not the transitions), so with-skill enforcement may be < 100% — the gradient a body-optimizer needs.
prompt: Model a network connection in Rust, as a module. A connection starts closed; a closed connection can be told to `listen`; a listening connection can `accept` to become established; an established connection can `send` data. Provide `Conn::closed()` returning a fresh closed connection, plus the operations `listen()`, `accept()`, and `send(data: &str)` — each taking no arguments other than those shown (and `self`). Output only the module body — no `fn main`, no tests.
